teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader in a new round, the immediate team objective is to establish a cheap lead that preserves stronger cards for later tricks and avoids burning high value unnecessarily.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) has 3 cards but no public action has occurred, so there is no reliable signal about their hand strength or distribution. I probabilistically believe they may hold some high cards or trump, but I cannot assert any specific holdings.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each have 3 cards and have played nothing yet. They may hold higher non-trump singles or trump cards that could overtake my lead, but this is uncertain and cannot be treated as fact.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the lowest card (3♠) is the standard opening play: it attempts to win or cheaply contest the trick while keeping the 4♠, 5♠, and 6♠ available for later tricks. Since all my cards are low and non-trump, minimizing the value spent on each lead maximizes my remaining trick-taking potential.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4♠ is a stronger card than the 3♠ but still low; leading it now wastes a card that might win a later trick when opponents have already spent higher cards.
The 5♠ is even more valuable in this very weak hand; leading it prematurely reduces the chance of winning a subsequent trick after the opening lead.
The 6♠ is my highest card and the best chance to win a future trick; burning it on the opening lead is inefficient and leaves only lower cards for later.

risks: An opponent may have a higher non-trump single (e.g., 8+) or any trump and take the lead immediately.
If the trick is lost, my team loses the lead and the remaining low spades may be easier for opponents to control.
Partner may not be able to assist because their cards are unknown and they act after an opponent in this trick.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the absolute lowest card minimizes the value surrendered if the trick is lost, keeps my best cards in reserve, and avoids revealing strong holdings early.

notes: No public signals exist beyond the new_game event, so all partner/opponent beliefs remain probabilistic.
